sections.plain_summary: Nickel sensitivity is an immune reaction to nickel — a metal found in food, jewelry, metal objects, and everyday items. For most people it causes contact skin rashes where nickel touches the skin. For a smaller group, swallowing nickel in food triggers whole-body symptoms including skin flares, gut problems, headaches, and fatigue. This more severe form is called Systemic Nickel Allergy Syndrome (SNAS).
sections.detailed_summary:
Nickel sensitivity is one of the most common forms of metal allergy worldwide. It is driven by a delayed-type hypersensitivity reaction (Type IV), where the immune system — specifically T-lymphocytes — recognizes nickel as a threat and mounts an inflammatory response. The classic presentation is allergic contact dermatitis: a localized, itchy, often weeping rash that appears where skin contacts nickel-containing metal, such as jewelry clasps, belt buckles, watch straps, or jeans buttons. This form is well-accepted and routinely diagnosed by dermatologists using patch testing.

A more controversial and often underdiagnosed presentation is Systemic Nickel Allergy Syndrome (SNAS), in which dietary nickel ingested through food and water triggers or worsens systemic symptoms. People with SNAS typically already have confirmed nickel contact allergy and then develop reactions to nickel eaten in food — leading to widespread skin involvement (generalized eczema, urticaria, dyshidrotic eczema on hands and feet), gastrointestinal symptoms (bloating, diarrhea, abdominal pain), and constitutional symptoms (fatigue, headache, cognitive fog). The gut plays a central role: nickel is absorbed through the gastrointestinal tract, and in sensitized individuals this can amplify immune activation beyond what skin contact alone produces. Diagnosis of SNAS requires a positive patch test plus symptom response to a low-nickel diet or an oral nickel challenge. Management involves reducing dietary nickel, minimizing skin and environmental contact, and in some cases using antihistamines or low-dose oral nickel tolerance protocols.

prevalence.summary: Nickel allergy is the most common metal allergy, affecting roughly 1 in 6 women and 1 in 25 men in Western countries.
prevalence.estimate: 10–20% of the general population have nickel contact allergy; SNAS affects an estimated 1–5% of the population
prevalence.notes: Prevalence estimates vary significantly by region and testing method. Women are disproportionately affected, largely due to higher rates of ear piercing (a major sensitization route). Patch test data from dermatology clinics skews toward already-symptomatic people, so population-wide figures may be lower. SNAS prevalence is less well established and is contested — some researchers consider it underdiagnosed, while others are skeptical of the systemic dietary link in milder cases.

subgroups.name: Allergic Contact Dermatitis (ACD) Only
subgroups.description: The classic, most common form. Reactions are limited to skin sites in direct contact with nickel-containing objects. No systemic or dietary component.
distinguishing_features: Rash appears only where metal touches skin (ears, wrists, abdomen, neck)
Patch test positive to nickel
No gut symptoms or constitutional symptoms
Symptoms resolve with avoidance of contact sources
Dietary nickel does not significantly worsen symptoms

subgroups.name: Systemic Nickel Allergy Syndrome (SNAS)
subgroups.description: A more severe form where dietary nickel ingestion triggers or worsens systemic symptoms beyond the contact site, including widespread skin involvement, gut symptoms, and fatigue.
distinguishing_features: Positive patch test to nickel is required for diagnosis
Symptoms worsen with high-nickel foods (oats, legumes, chocolate, whole grains, nuts)
Widespread or generalized eczema, not just contact sites
Gastrointestinal symptoms: bloating, diarrhea, abdominal cramping
Constitutional symptoms: fatigue, headache, brain fog
Low-nickel diet produces measurable symptom improvement
Oral nickel challenge can reproduce symptoms in a clinical setting

subgroups.name: Dyshidrotic Eczema (Pompholyx) Subtype
subgroups.description: A subgroup where nickel sensitivity manifests primarily as dyshidrotic eczema — deep, intensely itchy blisters on the palms and soles — often driven by dietary nickel.
distinguishing_features: Blistering eczema on palms, fingers, and/or soles of feet
Often triggered or worsened by sweating and dietary nickel
May flare with high-nickel foods even without obvious skin contact
Can be confused with fungal infection or irritant dermatitis
Responds to low-nickel diet in confirmed sensitized individuals

subgroups.name: Occupational Nickel Sensitivity
subgroups.description: Individuals sensitized or repeatedly exposed to nickel through their work environment (hairdressers, metalworkers, coin handlers, food service workers using stainless steel equipment).
distinguishing_features: Hands are the primary affected site
Occupational history is a key diagnostic clue
Higher total nickel exposure than average population
May require workplace accommodations or career change for meaningful relief
Risk of chronic hand eczema if exposure continues

symptoms.notes: Classic presentation at contact sites — earlobes, wrists, belt line, neck. Rash typically appears 12–48 hours after contact and can persist for days to weeks.

symptoms.symptom: Generalized eczema or widespread skin flares
symptoms.notes: More common in SNAS. Skin involvement extends beyond contact areas, often including trunk, limbs, and face.

symptoms.symptom: Dyshidrotic eczema (deep blisters on palms and soles)
symptoms.notes: Intensely itchy, deep-seated vesicles that may weep or become secondarily infected. Often worsened by dietary nickel.
In SNAS, dietary nickel can contribute to recurring or unexplained hives. Often not linked to nickel without targeted dietary history.
May precede visible rash. Can be severe enough to disrupt sleep.
Common in SNAS. Often worsens after meals high in nickel (whole grains, legumes, chocolate).
Can follow nickel-rich meals in sensitized individuals. Sometimes mistaken for IBS.
Intermittent cramping, particularly in the lower abdomen, reported in SNAS.
Less common but reported, particularly after high nickel intake.
Reported as a persistent background symptom in SNAS, worsened on high-nickel days.
Reported in SNAS, mechanism not fully established. May relate to systemic immune activation.
Less well-documented than skin and gut symptoms but reported by patients with SNAS.
Reported in more severe SNAS cases. Not a primary or well-established symptom.
Reported in some SNAS patients; less common than skin and gut symptoms.

key_patterns: Positive patch test to nickel sulfate with clinical symptoms consistent with ACD or SNAS
Skin symptoms at classic contact sites (earlobes, wrists, belt line) that resolve when nickel contact is removed
Symptom flares following meals high in nickel (oats, legumes, whole grains, chocolate, nuts) in someone with confirmed nickel allergy
Dyshidrotic eczema on hands/feet that worsens after dietary nickel without an obvious contact source
Widespread eczema or hives in a known nickel-allergic person that fails to respond to standard contact avoidance alone
Improvement of systemic symptoms on a 4–6 week low-nickel diet trial

at_home.name: Dimethylglyoxime (DMG) Spot Test
at_home.description: A simple chemical test to detect nickel release from metal objects. Available as test kits online. Apply the two reagents (dimethylglyoxime solution and ammonia solution) to a cotton swab and rub on the metal surface. A pink or red colour indicates nickel is leaching from the object. Useful for testing jewelry, belt buckles, watchbands, and other everyday metal items.
at_home.safety_notes: Safe for home use. Does not test for all nickel-releasing sources. A negative result does not guarantee zero nickel release — test multiple spots. Not a substitute for patch testing your skin's actual immune response.

at_home.name: Low-Nickel Diet Trial
at_home.description: Eliminate or significantly reduce high-nickel foods for 4–6 weeks and monitor symptom changes. High-nickel foods include: oats, whole wheat/rye, legumes (lentils, chickpeas, kidney beans, soybeans), nuts and seeds, chocolate and cocoa, shellfish, and leafy greens like spinach. Keep a food and symptom diary to identify patterns. If symptoms improve meaningfully, nickel may be a dietary contributor.
at_home.safety_notes: Ensure dietary balance when restricting multiple food groups — particularly fibre and protein sources. The low-nickel diet can be restrictive and difficult to sustain long-term. Do not self-diagnose SNAS from dietary response alone — confirm with a clinician. Reintroducing high-nickel foods to test response is informative but may cause flares.

at_home.name: Metal Contact Elimination
at_home.description: Systematically remove or replace nickel-releasing metal items in your daily life — earrings, necklaces, belts, watches, glasses frames, clothing fasteners, phone cases. Switch to surgical-grade stainless steel, titanium, niobium, gold (14k or higher), or sterling silver. Monitor skin symptoms over 2–4 weeks.
at_home.safety_notes: Gold-plated jewelry is not safe — the base metal may be nickel. Silver-plated jewelry may also contain nickel underneath. Look for 'nickel-free' or 'hypoallergenic' labeling, though these terms are not always regulated.

clinical.name: Patch Test (Epicutaneous Patch Test)
clinical.description: The standard diagnostic test for nickel contact allergy. A small amount of nickel sulfate (typically 5% in petrolatum) is applied to the back under adhesive patches, left in place for 48 hours, then read at 48 and 96 hours. A positive result (redness, papules, vesicles at the test site) confirms delayed-type hypersensitivity to nickel. Part of the standard European baseline series or North American Contact Dermatitis Group series.
clinical.availability: Performed by dermatologists, allergists, and contact dermatitis clinics. Widely available in mainstream dermatology. Results require clinical interpretation by a trained practitioner.

clinical.name: Oral Nickel Challenge Test
clinical.description: Used to confirm SNAS in people already known to be nickel-allergic on patch testing. A controlled oral dose of nickel (typically 0.3–2.24 mg nickel sulfate) is administered under clinical supervision. Symptom response — particularly skin and gut symptoms — is monitored over 24–48 hours. A positive challenge supports SNAS diagnosis.
clinical.availability: Performed in specialist allergy or dermatology units. Not widely available in all countries. Requires clinical oversight — not a test to attempt independently.

clinical.name: Lymphocyte Transformation Test (LTT)
clinical.description: A blood test that measures T-cell proliferation in response to nickel exposure in vitro. Proposed as an alternative or complement to patch testing, particularly useful when patch testing cannot be performed (e.g., active widespread eczema). Less well-validated than patch testing for contact allergy specifically.
clinical.availability: Available at select specialist immunology or allergy labs. Not standard in most dermatology practices. May need to be specifically requested or sought at academic medical centers.

clinical.name: Urine Nickel Levels
clinical.description: Measures total nickel excretion in urine, which reflects systemic nickel burden from dietary and environmental exposure. Not diagnostic of nickel allergy on its own but used in research settings and some specialist SNAS protocols to quantify exposure and monitor dietary interventions.
clinical.availability: Available through occupational health labs and some specialist allergy units. Not routinely ordered in standard clinical practice for SNAS.

related_conditions.name: Irritable Bowel Syndrome (IBS)
related_conditions.explanation: SNAS gut symptoms — bloating, cramping, diarrhea — closely resemble IBS. Some patients diagnosed with IBS may have an unrecognized dietary nickel component. Studies have found nickel allergy rates higher in IBS patients than controls, though causality is uncertain.

related_conditions.name: Atopic Dermatitis (Eczema)
related_conditions.explanation: People with atopic dermatitis have a disrupted skin barrier that makes nickel sensitization more likely. Nickel allergy is more prevalent in atopic individuals, and ongoing dietary nickel can worsen atopic eczema in sensitized people.

related_conditions.name: Fibromyalgia
related_conditions.explanation: Fatigue, widespread pain, and cognitive symptoms in SNAS can overlap with fibromyalgia presentations. Some patients with refractory fibromyalgia-like symptoms have been found to have SNAS, though this overlap is not well-established in large studies.

related_conditions.name: Small Intestinal Bacterial Overgrowth (SIBO)
related_conditions.explanation: Altered gut motility and barrier function associated with nickel-related gut inflammation may predispose to or coexist with SIBO. Both conditions share overlapping gut symptoms, complicating diagnosis.

related_conditions.name: Cobalt and Chromium Allergy
related_conditions.explanation: Nickel allergy frequently co-occurs with cobalt and chromium allergy, as these metals share similar industrial sources and often appear together in consumer products and alloys. Patients sensitized to nickel should also be tested for these metals.

related_conditions.name: Oral Allergy Syndrome
related_conditions.explanation: Oral symptoms after eating certain foods can occur in both oral allergy syndrome (driven by cross-reactive plant proteins) and SNAS (driven by dietary nickel content). Distinguishing them requires careful dietary history and allergy testing.

related_conditions.name: Celiac Disease
related_conditions.explanation: Celiac disease and SNAS share high-nickel foods (whole grains, oats) as dietary triggers. Celiac disease also increases intestinal permeability, which may enhance nickel absorption and worsen systemic reactions. The two can coexist and must be distinguished through appropriate testing.

strategies.name: Low-Nickel Diet
strategies.description: Reducing dietary nickel intake is the cornerstone of SNAS management. The diet involves avoiding or minimizing high-nickel foods: oats, whole wheat and rye bread, legumes, nuts, seeds, chocolate, cocoa, shellfish, canned foods (nickel leaches from cans), and some leafy greens. Lower-nickel options include white rice, white bread, meat, fish, eggs, most fruits, and dairy. Cooking in stainless steel pots can also increase food nickel content — ceramic or glass cookware reduces this.
strategies.notes: Evidence supports symptom improvement in confirmed SNAS, but the diet is restrictive and eliminates many nutritious whole foods. Work with a dietitian familiar with SNAS to maintain nutritional adequacy. The diet is most effective as part of a confirmed SNAS diagnosis, not as a first-line self-treatment without patch testing.

strategies.name: Contact Nickel Avoidance
strategies.description: Replace nickel-releasing metal items with nickel-free alternatives: surgical steel, titanium, niobium, 14k+ gold, or sterling silver jewelry. Cover metal buttons or rivets in clothing with fabric tape or nail varnish. Choose watchstraps in leather, cloth, or plastic. Test items with a DMG spot test before use. This is the primary management for ACD-only cases and an important adjunct in SNAS.
strategies.notes: EU regulations (REACH) limit nickel release from consumer products — goods sold in the EU are more reliably tested. In other regions, 'nickel-free' labeling is less regulated.

strategies.name: Antihistamines
strategies.description: Non-sedating oral antihistamines (e.g., cetirizine, loratadine) can reduce itching and urticarial symptoms. Useful for acute flares and as ongoing symptom management. They do not address the underlying immune mechanism but provide meaningful relief for many patients.
strategies.notes: Most effective for urticarial and itch symptoms. Less effective for vesicular or lichenified eczema. Available over the counter in most countries.

strategies.name: Topical Corticosteroids
strategies.description: Moderate to potent topical corticosteroids (e.g., betamethasone valerate, mometasone furoate) applied to affected skin areas reduce localized inflammation during flares. Short-course use for acute episodes is standard management.
strategies.notes: Long-term or widespread use carries risks: skin thinning, striae, adrenal suppression with very potent agents. Use the lowest effective potency for the shortest necessary duration. Avoid on face without specialist guidance.

strategies.name: Topical Calcineurin Inhibitors (Tacrolimus/Pimecrolimus)
strategies.description: Non-steroidal topical immunomodulators that reduce skin inflammation without the skin-thinning risk of steroids. Useful for sensitive areas (face, eyelids, skin folds) or when steroid-sparing is needed for longer-term management.
strategies.notes: May cause initial burning or stinging on application. Prescription-only in most countries. Not first-line but a valuable option for steroid-sensitive areas.

strategies.name: Oral Nickel Desensitization (Tolerance Induction)
strategies.description: A specialist protocol involving controlled, gradually increasing oral doses of nickel sulfate to reduce immune reactivity over time — similar in concept to allergen immunotherapy. Some studies show improvement in SNAS symptoms with protocols lasting several months.
strategies.notes: Not widely available and not standardized. Should only be undertaken under specialist supervision. Evidence is promising but comes from small studies. Not appropriate for self-administration.

strategies.name: Emollients and Barrier Repair
strategies.description: Regular use of fragrance-free emollients (moisturizers) helps repair the skin barrier and reduces susceptibility to contact irritants and allergens. Apply frequently, especially after handwashing. Choose products free of fragrances, preservatives, and lanolin, which can be co-sensitizers.
strategies.notes: An underutilized but important part of everyday skin management. Does not treat the allergy itself but significantly reduces flare frequency and severity.

strategies.name: Vitamin C with High-Nickel Meals
strategies.description: Vitamin C (ascorbic acid) has been shown to reduce intestinal absorption of nickel when taken with meals. Some SNAS protocols recommend 500–1000mg with main meals to reduce the systemic nickel burden from food.
strategies.notes: Evidence is from small studies and expert opinion rather than large RCTs. Well-tolerated at standard doses. High doses (above 2000mg/day) can cause gastrointestinal upset. Discuss with a clinician before starting.

strategies.name: Iron Supplementation (if iron deficient)
strategies.description: Iron deficiency increases intestinal nickel absorption — the same transporter (DMT1) handles both. Correcting iron deficiency may reduce nickel uptake in the gut and lessen systemic exposure. A ferritin level should be checked before supplementing.
strategies.notes: Do not supplement iron without confirming deficiency through blood testing. Excessive iron supplementation has its own risks. This strategy is most relevant for people with confirmed low iron.

strategies.name: Stress Reduction
strategies.description: Psychological stress is a known trigger for eczema flares and can worsen immune dysregulation. Techniques such as mindfulness, exercise, adequate sleep, and cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT) can reduce flare frequency and severity.
strategies.notes: Does not address the nickel sensitivity directly but is a meaningful adjunct to management. Particularly relevant for patients with chronic relapsing skin symptoms.

things_to_avoid: Nickel-releasing jewelry, particularly in pierced ears — piercings are a major sensitization route and ongoing exposure source
Stainless steel cookware for acidic foods (e.g., tomato sauce) — acid increases nickel leaching; use glass or ceramic alternatives
Canned foods — nickel leaches from metal cans, particularly with acidic or high-water content foods
High-nickel foods eaten in large quantities: oats, whole wheat, rye, legumes, dark chocolate, nuts, seeds, shellfish
First-run tap water in the morning — water standing overnight in metal pipes has higher nickel content; run the tap for 30 seconds first
Prolonged contact with metal-handled tools, utensils, or equipment at work without protective gloves
Wet work without gloves — repeated water exposure removes skin oils and increases nickel penetration through the skin barrier
Fragrance or preservative-containing skincare products — these can irritate already-sensitized skin and cause co-sensitization
Self-diagnosing SNAS and eliminating major food groups without professional guidance — risks nutritional deficiencies and unnecessary restriction
Assuming 'hypoallergenic' or 'nickel-free' jewelry labeling is always accurate — verify with a DMG spot test

uncomfortable_truths.truth: For many people with SNAS, meaningful improvement requires giving up staple, nutritious foods — whole grains, legumes, nuts — indefinitely. This is not a short-term fix.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: These are foods universally promoted as healthy. Patients struggle to accept that foods widely regarded as beneficial are making them ill, and the diet feels punishing and socially isolating.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: Nickel sensitivity cannot be cured. It is a lifelong immune alteration that can only be managed, not reversed, in most people.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: Patients understandably hope that identifying the trigger means solving the problem. The reality that avoidance management is permanent is discouraging.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: Most mainstream doctors — including many dermatologists — are not familiar with SNAS. Patients often have to advocate firmly for appropriate testing and dietary guidance.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: People reasonably expect their doctor to recognize and test for a well-documented condition. Repeated dismissal erodes trust and delays diagnosis, sometimes by years.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: Ear piercing — especially multiple or early childhood piercings — is the most common way women become sensitized to nickel in the first place. Piercings that seemed fine at the time can be the origin of a lifelong immune sensitivity.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: Piercings are normalized, and the connection between an old piercing and current widespread eczema or gut symptoms is not intuitive. People don't want to feel that a cosmetic choice years ago caused a chronic condition.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: Total nickel avoidance is essentially impossible. The goal is meaningful reduction in exposure, not elimination. Expecting perfection leads to unnecessary anxiety and dietary restriction.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: Once sensitized individuals learn how ubiquitous nickel is, it can feel overwhelming and lead to disordered eating or extreme restriction that exceeds what is clinically necessary.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: The evidence base for SNAS, including the precise dietary thresholds that trigger symptoms, remains incomplete. Some symptoms attributed to dietary nickel may have other causes.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: After years of unexplained symptoms, a diagnosis of SNAS brings relief and a clear framework. Patients may resist the idea that the picture is more complex and that nickel is not necessarily responsible for every symptom.

key_levers.lever: Confirm the diagnosis with patch testing before making major dietary changes
key_levers.why_it_matters: A low-nickel diet is demanding and nutritionally restrictive. Without confirmed nickel allergy on patch test, you may be restricting your diet unnecessarily — or missing the actual cause of symptoms. Dietary trials without diagnostic confirmation are unreliable.
key_levers.how_to_start: Ask your GP or dermatologist for a referral to a contact dermatitis clinic or allergist for patch testing with the standard or extended metal series.
key_levers.timeline: Patch testing takes 4–5 days (application, first read, second read); getting an appointment may take weeks to months depending on your location.

key_levers.lever: Eliminate the highest-impact nickel contact sources first
key_levers.why_it_matters: Earrings, especially in pierced ears, and frequently worn jewelry at the wrists and neck can deliver continuous nickel exposure to the immune system. Removing these sources often produces dramatic skin improvement without any dietary change.
key_levers.how_to_start: Test all jewelry and metal accessories with a DMG spot test kit. Replace positive items with titanium, niobium, or solid 14k+ gold alternatives. Cover metal clothing fasteners temporarily with fabric or nail polish while sourcing alternatives.
key_levers.timeline: Skin improvement at contact sites often visible within 2–4 weeks of removing the source.

key_levers.lever: Implement a structured low-nickel diet with dietitian support
key_levers.why_it_matters: For SNAS, dietary nickel is a systemic trigger that contact avoidance alone cannot address. A properly structured low-nickel diet reduces total body nickel burden and can significantly improve widespread skin symptoms, gut symptoms, and fatigue.
key_levers.how_to_start: Keep a detailed food and symptom diary for 2 weeks before starting. Then work with a registered dietitian experienced in food allergy or elimination diets to implement the low-nickel diet safely while maintaining nutritional adequacy.
key_levers.timeline: Meaningful symptom improvement typically within 4–8 weeks of adherence to a low-nickel diet.

key_levers.why_it_matters: Iron deficiency directly increases intestinal nickel absorption through shared transport mechanisms. Correcting iron deficiency reduces nickel uptake from food — potentially reducing systemic nickel burden without additional dietary restriction.
key_levers.how_to_start: Request a full blood count and serum ferritin test from your GP. If iron or ferritin is low, discuss appropriate supplementation or dietary iron sources.
key_levers.timeline: Iron levels typically improve over 2–3 months of supplementation; effect on nickel absorption would follow.

key_levers.lever: Protect and restore the skin barrier daily
key_levers.why_it_matters: A compromised skin barrier makes the skin more permeable to nickel and other irritants, perpetuating a cycle of sensitization and inflammation. Consistent barrier support reduces flare frequency even when exposure cannot be fully controlled.
key_levers.how_to_start: Apply a fragrance-free, preservative-minimal emollient (e.g., Cetaphil, QV, Aveeno Eczema Therapy, or a simple petroleum-based product) twice daily and immediately after hand washing. Choose products without fragrance, lanolin, or common preservatives.
key_levers.timeline: Skin texture and resilience typically improves over 4–8 weeks of consistent use.

common_mistakes: Starting a strict low-nickel diet before confirming the diagnosis with patch testing — this risks unnecessary long-term restriction and can mask other diagnoses
Focusing exclusively on diet while continuing to wear nickel-releasing jewelry — both routes of exposure must be addressed simultaneously for meaningful improvement
Expecting the low-nickel diet to eliminate symptoms entirely — total nickel avoidance is impossible and aiming for perfection leads to disordered eating and persistent distress
Dismissing systemic symptoms (gut, fatigue, headache) as unrelated to nickel allergy, delaying SNAS diagnosis by years
Using 'hypoallergenic' or 'nickel-free' labeled jewelry without verifying with a DMG spot test — these labels are not always reliable
Stopping topical treatments as soon as the skin improves, then experiencing rapid relapse — barrier repair and consistent emollient use must be ongoing, not just during flares
Attributing all ongoing symptoms to SNAS without investigating other concurrent conditions (atopic dermatitis, IBS, celiac disease) that may require separate treatment
Cooking acidic foods in stainless steel pots and pans while following a low-nickel diet — this can significantly increase dietary nickel intake and undermine dietary efforts

specialists.role: Performs patch testing to confirm nickel allergy, diagnoses ACD, and can evaluate for SNAS. Often the primary clinician for diagnosis and prescription treatment of skin manifestations.
specialists.notes: Seek a dermatologist with a specific interest in contact dermatitis or patch testing — not all dermatologists perform or interpret patch tests routinely. Academic medical centers and specialist contact dermatitis clinics offer the most comprehensive patch test panels.

specialists.role: Can perform or supervise patch testing, conduct oral nickel challenge tests, and manage systemic allergic responses. Useful for complex SNAS presentations or when multiple metal sensitivities are suspected.
specialists.notes: More familiar with systemic allergic mechanisms than many dermatologists. Can also evaluate for coexisting food allergies or atopic conditions.

specialists.type: Registered Dietitian (Food Allergy / Elimination Diet Specialist)
specialists.role: Guides implementation of a nutritionally adequate low-nickel diet. Helps identify hidden dietary sources of nickel, plan meals, and monitor for nutritional deficiencies during restriction.
specialists.notes: Expertise in food allergy elimination diets is more relevant than general dietetics. Ask specifically about experience with metal-related dietary restrictions or SNAS.

specialists.role: Relevant for SNAS patients with significant gut symptoms. Helps rule out or treat comorbid conditions including IBS, SIBO, celiac disease, or inflammatory bowel disease that may coexist or be confused with SNAS.
specialists.notes: May not be familiar with SNAS specifically. Bring documentation of nickel allergy and SNAS literature to inform the consultation.

specialists.role: Important for patients whose nickel exposure is primarily occupational. Assesses workplace exposure, advises on protective measures, and can support workplace accommodation requests or compensation claims.
specialists.notes: Particularly relevant for metalworkers, hairdressers, healthcare workers, and others with chronic occupational nickel contact.

patient_communities.name: r/eczema
patient_communities.notes: Broad eczema community with regular discussion of nickel allergy triggers, low-nickel diet experiences, and product recommendations. Useful for practical peer advice, though medical accuracy varies.

patient_communities.name: Nickel Allergy Support Group
patient_communities.notes: Search Facebook for 'Nickel Allergy' or 'Nickel Allergy Support Group' — several active groups exist. Members share food lists, product recommendations, and experiences with the low-nickel diet. Quality of medical information varies; useful for lived experience and practical tips.

patient_communities.name: Inspire Allergy and Asthma Community
patient_communities.notes: While primarily latex-focused, Inspire hosts allergy communities where nickel and metal allergy topics appear. More moderated than Facebook groups.

patient_communities.name: HealthUnlocked Allergy UK Community
patient_communities.notes: UK-based patient community with involvement from Allergy UK. Covers nickel allergy among other allergic conditions. More likely to have UK-relevant medical guidance and specialist referral advice.

learning_resources.title: Systemic Nickel Allergy Syndrome: Epidemiology, Pathogenesis, Clinical Features, and Diagnosis
learning_resources.author_or_source: Ricciardi L, Carni A et al. — published in International Journal of Immunopathology and Pharmacology
learning_resources.description: A foundational review paper on SNAS covering its mechanisms, diagnostic criteria, and clinical presentation. Useful for understanding the scientific basis of the condition and for sharing with clinicians unfamiliar with SNAS.

learning_resources.title: Nickel Allergy — DermNet NZ
learning_resources.description: A clear, evidence-based overview of nickel allergy covering contact dermatitis, SNAS, patch testing, and management. Reliable, well-referenced, and accessible to non-specialists. A good starting point for newly diagnosed patients.

learning_resources.title: Nickel Allergy — American Academy of Dermatology
learning_resources.description: A patient-facing overview from a major professional dermatology organization. Covers symptoms, triggers, avoidance, and when to see a dermatologist. Conservative and mainstream in its scope.

learning_resources.title: Low Nickel Diet — Allergy UK
learning_resources.description: A practical, patient-friendly resource specifically on the low-nickel diet from a reputable UK charity. Includes food lists, cooking tips, and guidance on hidden nickel sources. Particularly useful for UK-based patients.

learning_resources.title: Contact Dermatitis (5th Edition)
learning_resources.author_or_source: Johansen JD, Mahler V, Lepoittevin J-P, Frosch PJ (editors)
learning_resources.description: The definitive medical textbook on contact dermatitis, including comprehensive coverage of nickel allergy, patch testing methodology, and occupational exposure. Written for clinicians but valuable for informed patients who want authoritative depth.

learning_resources.title: The Eczema Detox
learning_resources.description: A popular patient-facing book that covers low-chemical diets for eczema, including sections on nickel and other dietary triggers. Practical and accessible. Note that some recommendations go beyond mainstream evidence — treat as a practical guide rather than a medical text.

learning_resources.title: European Society of Contact Dermatitis (ESCD) Guidelines on Nickel
learning_resources.description: Clinical guidelines from the leading European professional body for contact dermatitis. Covers patch testing standards, management recommendations, and SNAS diagnostic criteria. Useful for clinicians and informed patients who want to understand the current clinical standard of care.

sections.relevance_signal: Symptoms worse on a **vegan or wholefood diet** heavy in oats, legumes, nuts, seeds, chocolate. Two-phase reaction: gut symptoms at 1–3h then fatigue and joint aching 24–48h later.
